topic: Two coworkers discover they went to the same summer camp
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed, a grating soundtrack to Clara’s mounting anxiety. She kept fiddling with the edge of her mousepad, her knuckles white against the plastic. It had been three weeks since the realization hit her—a shared, improbable history with Ben. They had been assigned to the same project team, a coincidence that felt less like fate and more like a cruel cosmic joke. She replayed the faded photos on her phone, the sun-bleached pictures of canoes and muddy fields from that summer. A tightness coiled in her chest, making each breath shallow. She needed a distraction, anything to quiet the frantic thrumming beneath her ribs. Then, a casual comment from Mark about a forgotten summer camp song sent a jolt through her. Her gaze snapped up, meeting Ben’s across the cubicle farm. His eyes widened, a flicker of recognition—a shared, unspoken memory—passed between them before he quickly looked away, a flush creeping up his neck. Clara felt a sudden, overwhelming need to disappear, to vanish into the beige carpet.

[story 2]

"Did you ever go to Camp Whispering Pines?" Liam asked, his voice too bright, too eager. He leaned against the water cooler, his posture radiating an almost brittle energy. Sarah felt the blood drain from her face. The memory, sharp and unwelcome, clawed at the inside of her mind. She had spent the last few evenings staring blankly at spreadsheets, unable to focus on quarterly reports. Every time she thought of that summer—the sticky humidity, the smell of pine needles, the relentless, echoing laughter—a knot formed just beneath her sternum. "I... I think so," she managed, her voice sounding thin and reedy. She gripped her coffee mug so tightly her fingers ached, the ceramic digging into her palm. Liam’s enthusiasm was infectious, yet it only amplified her internal turmoil. He seemed entirely unaware of the seismic shift happening within her, oblivious to the way her gaze kept drifting toward the window, searching for something that wasn't there.

[story 3]

Marcus paced the length of his small apartment, the worn floorboards protesting under his hurried steps. He kept running a hand over his jaw, the stubble scratching against his palm. The silence of the evening was oppressive, amplifying the frantic monologue running through his head. He had been trying to compartmentalize, to shove the summer memories into a sealed box, but they kept leaking out, vivid and inescapable. He remembered the sheer exhaustion of those days, the feeling of being utterly adrift under a vast, indifferent sky. A sudden, sharp memory of a shared campfire, the way the stars had looked—it brought a tremor to his hands. He slammed his fist softly onto the kitchen counter, the dull thud echoing his inner agitation. He needed an anchor, a tangible thing to hold onto, anything to stop the relentless churning inside him.
